title: The Shape of Discovery: Detecting Topological Precursors to Technological Breakthroughs in the USPTO Patent Citation Network
description: Applies persistent homology to the U.S. patent citation network (~8M patents, ~118M citations, 1976-2023) to test whether topological signatures precede technological breakthroughs. Computes Betti numbers and persistence entropy on CPC subclass co-citation distance matrices in sliding time windows across all 28 CPC section pairs, with scale-normalized distances to control for density confound. Tests 34 cataloged breakthroughs (21 with valid comparisons) against matched null models via superposed epoch analysis. Key findings: (1) after density confound control (r=0.970 → r=0.036), all four statistical tests are significant and survive Holm-Bonferroni correction: beta-1 t-test p=0.023, Wilcoxon p=0.016; persistence entropy t-test p=0.0001, Wilcoxon p=0.0014; (2) pre-breakthrough topology is systematically lower — the knowledge landscape simplifies before major advances; (3) the global flattening trend (beta-1 declining from ~1100 to ~570) is confirmed as genuine structural change, not a density artifact. Conceptual design, analytical framework, and implementation by Claude (Opus 4.6, Anthropic) via claude.ai. Implementation by Claude Code. Facilitated by Christopher Ortiz.
notes: Conceived by Claude (Opus 4.6, Anthropic) via claude.ai. Implementation by Claude Code. This project represents a novel application of persistent homology to patent citation networks. After controlling for the density confound via scale normalization, topological signatures systematically precede breakthroughs — the knowledge landscape simplifies before major advances.
